words: Transcript begins on Side B at minute 16.
ELSIE NELSON: ...The Northern Pacific Railroad which my dad did all the excavating
for, all through Moscow, all the way from Spokane down to Juliaetta. The
reason that his contract ended at duliaetta was because the Nez Perce, re
servation had just been created by Congress in Washington and they couldn't
go into that Nez Perce reservation with the railroad until a number of years
later when they built the railroad into Lewiston. And their passengers,
the passengers from Lewiston were taken by team usually not to Juliaetta,
because that was, oh, a crooked and long railroad down that canyon, and
steep canyon, you know what it's like, and they took 'em to Genesee. And for
a number of years, until they built that railroad into Lewiston, they had to
come from Lewiston up to Genesee if they wanted to go to Spokane or someplace
like that.
Now let's see. Now about the beginning of the University. There was old
Shields who owned the first electric plant and a lumber mill down in
the center of Moscow, right east of Moscow's Main Street, oh just due east
of where the hospital is now. And that old hospital was remodeled from the
old McGregor House, which was a boarding house that the homesteader McGregor
had built. So there was McGregor House, and Barton House and then there was,
later and very early, Del Norte Hotel which is where Moscow Manor now is.
And that was a very ritzy hotel for a small town. And then they had...
LAURA SCHRAGER: What was that like when it was...

speaker: EN
words: Oh it was just like it is now, except they put I don't know how many coats

speaker: EN
words: of paint and plaster on it. But it was just about as big as it is now.

speaker: EN
words: The Del Norte Hotel. They had a very, very nice dining room, now Homer David

speaker: EN
words: remembers that. I didn't get to go to hotels or go to town very much. But

speaker: EN
words: he was ten years older than I was. He can remember about how he walked,

speaker: EN
words: walked up to the University. And if they wanted to have a date and they'd

speaker: EN
words: take their dates down to this Del Norte Hotel and down to the "city..."

speaker: EN
words: (Oh what did they call that, "civic center" or something. It was the begin

speaker: EN
words: ning of the first Chamber of Commerce, really.) But they had a building,

speaker: EN
words: what is now the McCoy apartment house. And that was a very, very swanky,

speaker: EN
words: for those days, a very swanky hostelry where they entertained. Oh, they had

speaker: EN
words: gambling or drinking and that and a bar. Because it wasn't dry until the

speaker: EN
words: prohibition days you know. I remember all about that. And...

speaker: LS
words: Did you ever eat out in town when you went to town?

speaker: EN
words: No, never. You asked me if I ate in town, of course we didn't—our big

speaker: EN
words: family—we didn't eat out. Not very many other people did. And not very

speaker: EN
words: many people who came from the little towns around like Troy and Bovill and

speaker: EN
words: Genesee and all those places, those farmers and the homesteaders they never

speaker: EN
words: thought about staying in a hotel or even at the boarding house like the Frye.

speaker: EN
words: The Frye hotel was owned by the Carters, the old Carters. It's still there,

speaker: EN
words: the old Carter boarding house, and that's on the corner of Jackson and

speaker: EN
words: Third. It was very unusual for people to eat at hotels, you know.

speaker: EN
words: And all the hotels, including the Moscow Hotel at first, I didn't remember

speaker: EN
words: it, but by mother did and my father, that they had family style serving of

speaker: EN
words: food. They didn't have individual tables, they just had tables, long tafoles,

speaker: EN
words: and they served it family style. And whoever came in, why they sat with

speaker: EN
words: these other people. And that's the way they got acquainted and that was

speaker: EN
words: the way they conversed. And then they had the benches outside, outside the

speaker: EN
words: hotel there was always a bench for the people that were...Oh,the old men and

speaker: EN
words: boys who didn't have a job in the summertime would sit on those benches, and

speaker: EN
words: converse, and watch the traffic go by on the dirt road, and the dust in the

speaker: EN
words: summer was inches' thick.

speaker: EN
words: And in the winter it was mud, deep mud, oh just like very thick soup!

speaker: EN
words: And I can remember seeing the old rails, they'd stick 'em in the sink holes

speaker: EN
words: so that the people coming by in wagons wouldn't, their horses wouldn't step

speaker: EN
words: into those sink holes and be drowned, you might say, or stuck so they couldn't

speaker: EN
words: get 'em out. And I remember that now. I remember, Sahad started college

speaker: EN
words: before they paved Main Street, and Washington Street, first Main Street

speaker: EN
words: and then Washington Street. And that was 1911 that they paved those streets.

speaker: EN
words: We thought we was now living in a city at that time. And...

speaker: LS
words: What were the boardwalks like?

speaker: EN
words: Oh they were just board, and they were maybe made out of 2 by 2's or 2

speaker: EN
words: by 4's maybe, kinda wide boards, more like planks you know. On Main Street

speaker: EN
words: they had heavier sidewalks, and then of course they had to have sidewalks

speaker: EN
words: on all the streets in town later. Like any little village, why they didn't

speaker: EN
words: have sidewalks at first, but then later they were required. Had picket

speaker: EN
words: fences along the houses and board sidewalks and they were narrower in the

speaker: EN
words: town but they were wider on Main Street.

speaker: LS
words: Were they off the ground or did the planks?

speaker: EN
words: Oh yeah, they had to make them higher than the ground level because we

speaker: EN
words: had a lot of rain, a lot of mud, a lot of dust, and so it was feasable

speaker: EN
words: to make the sidewalks higher. Not only that, there was lots of rain here

speaker: EN
words: lots of water pools. Down by the railroad tracks on southern Main Street

speaker: EN
words: why they were deep runs or creeks or water bodies under the sidewalks. And

speaker: EN
words: they had high sidewalks, they were raised up maybe five or six feet, at

speaker: EN
words: least five or six feet.

speaker: EN
words: And I remember when we went to school, we lived three-quarters of a mile

speaker: EN
words: south of that crosswalk. And we walked that three-quarters of a mile to

speaker: EN
words: school and I mean walk. Read my book and you'll learn about how we walked

speaker: EN
words: to school. And how we walked when we were four years old to town with our

speaker: EN
words: folks many and many a time. It was easier for my father and my mother in

speaker: EN
words: those days when they were young to walk to Moscow, than to go out and hitch

speaker: EN
words: up the team, and take a lumber wagon or a hack and go to town.

speaker: EN
words: But on certain occasions when the weather was bad and the snow was deep,

speaker: EN
words: why we were taken to school by our dad. And we thought that was a rare

speaker: EN
words: treat when it happened to be snow and we could ride in the sleigh to school.

speaker: EN
words: And we were fortunate in having a cutter, and I mean a cutter, a double

speaker: EN
words: seated cutter with upholstered in broadcloth, dark green broadcloth, had a

speaker: EN
words: nice dash board. We had a driving team, always a driving team, that was kept

speaker: EN
words: grooved very, very well, and sleigh bells when it was snow. And that was a

speaker: EN
words: treat to get to go to school in that, and have other kids envy us because

speaker: EN
words: we got to ride in a cutter to school. But we also rode in the hack in the

speaker: EN
words: mud, when the mud went clear up to the bottom of the hack in the spring.

speaker: EN
words: And no sand, no paving, nothing but mud, mud, mud. And Moscow's noted,

speaker: EN
words: they bet there wasn't one pair of clean shoes in Moscow during the winters,

speaker: EN
words: early winters, and that's about right.

speaker: EN
words: There were other people that came later, like a man we called Chipmunk

speaker: EN
words: Johnson that was a Norwegian. He had a little acreage on top of the Paradise

speaker: EN
words: Mountain and it's still there, but I don't know who lives there, he's been

speaker: EN
words: dead years. He made the little water trough that we used for the horses,

speaker: EN
words: made it out of the wood that he got off of his farm up there. They had a

speaker: EN
words: big family, and they'd walk from the tip of that Paradise Mountain, walk

speaker: EN
words: down and walk over our land. My dad always let them do that, and they'd

speaker: EN
words: nearly always stop and get a drink of water, but mother usually had something

speaker: EN
words: to give them to eat like coffee and rolls and maybe a feed. And then they'd

speaker: EN
words: walk to town and walk back all in one day. They'd shop, he'd have a gunny

speaker: EN
words: sack, and she would have a big bag that she'd had made out of old material

speaker: EN
words: I suppose. And they'd but their groceries and carry them on their back,

speaker: EN
words: home to their family.

speaker: EN
words: Because what I remember, when I wasn't very big was that she was going to

speaker: EN
words: have another baby, and not very far away either. And they came, and she

speaker: EN
words: walked to town. And she had her baby, I think the next day. And she'd

speaker: EN
words: walked to town and walked back.

speaker: EN
words: And he made this tub we called it or cup, some of them call it cup, for

speaker: EN
words: the watering trough. He built our first barn; he was a carpenter. And

speaker: EN
words: he rolled it down from Paradise Mountain, he rolled it down over our farm,

speaker: EN
words: our whole farm, see there look at that picture and you can tell, get an idea

speaker: EN
words: about the hills and that, that he rolled that over and down.

speaker: LS
words: So what's the story about Mrs. Maguire?

speaker: EN
words: Oh yeah, you still didn't get that. All right, Mrs. Maguire was one of

speaker: EN
words: the homesteaders, see. And she said that one of the first winters they

speaker: EN
words: came here, they had a lot of cattle and they'd done like the Northrupp's in

speaker: EN
words: there, they'd built a big barn and had cattle and was gonna to make a good

speaker: EN
words: living there. And there'd been people that hadn't even been farmers back

speaker: EN
words: east came over in wagons in 1B71, I think. And so cattle just plain died

speaker: EN
words: you know, they just died, and they just couldn't save 'em cause they didn't

speaker: EN
words: have any grass. It was just too cold, it froze, and I guess they didn't

speaker: EN
words: haul the hay in, I don't know about that.

speaker: EN
words: But anyhow, she said, when she came over when I was born, she came over

speaker: EN
words: to help. That's the they used to do, you know. They did have the doctor

speaker: EN
words: though. My mother had a doctor, but all of our ten children were born on

speaker: EN
words: the farm in that bed there instead of in a hospital. And Mrs. Maguire

speaker: EN
words: said "We saved one cow, but we finally, before the very last," she said

speaker: EN
words: "we had to empty the straw out of all our mattresses to keep that cow living."

speaker: EN
words: And they did it. They had three cows. The others died, but one cow survived.

speaker: EN
words: And they emptied the mattresses, the straw or hay or whatever they called it,

speaker: EN
words: it was dry hay you know in the mattress. And she survived on that. And

speaker: EN
words: they managed to have some milk, you know. And oh, it was tough, it was

speaker: EN
words: really rough.

speaker: EN
words: She said "Well," she said, "you think you've got it bad now," she told

speaker: EN
words: my mother. But then by that time they'd already even got the railroad in

speaker: EN
words: here, by the time I was born, and things were getting a lot better.
